01a_quotes.title: The Impact of AI on Developer Productivity: Evidence from GitHub Copilot

study_design: We conducted a controlled experiment to measure the productivity impact of using GitHub Copilot in programming tasks.
The experiment began on May 15, 2022 and ended on June 20, 2022, right before GitHub Copilot became generally available.
We recruited 95 professional programmers through Upwork, a freelancing platform.
Once participants signed the contract, they were randomly split into control and treatment groups.
Before we began recruitment, we received approval for the study from the Microsoft Research Ethics Review Board.
Using a standardized task provides us with precise measures of performance as it is difficult to measure productivity of software developers.
To administer the task, we used GitHub Classroom, a platform for teachers to issue and grade coding assignments.
In this way, we accurately measured the timing and completion for each participant.
task: Recruited software developers were asked to implement an HTTP server in JavaScript as quickly as possible.
Participants were instructed to write an HTTP server in JavaScript—the treatment group would use GitHub Copilot to complete the task, while the control group would not.

Besides the use of GitHub Copilot in the treated group, participants were unconstrained in their software development —they could use any sources of information as they normally do, such as internet search and Stack Overflow.
The instructions gave participants a link to a particular GitHub Classroom instance with a single assignment referencing a template repository.
When joining the assignment, participants received a personal copy of the template repository, with the task description (shown in Figure 4) and a skeleton codebase for participants to build upon.
We included a test suite in the repository, comprising twelve checks for submission correctness.
If a submission passes, all twelve tests we counted are successfully completed.
Participants could see the tests but were unable to alter them.
When participants committed and pushed their changes to GitHub, GitHub Classroom ran the test suite on their submission and reported the number of passing tests.
Participants could push as often as they pleased, automatically logging a timestamp each time.
The time elapsed between the timestamp of repository creation and the timestamp of the first commit to successfully pass all 12 tests was counted as the participant's task completion time.
population: A total of 166 offers were sent during the experiment, and 95 were accepted.
The 95 developers were randomly assigned into control and treated groups, with 45 in the treated group and 50 in control.
Thirty-five developers from both the treated and control groups completed the task and survey.
Most of the participants are in the age group of 25-34 and come from India and Pakistan.

This group of participants is also characterized by relatively lower income (median yearly income between $10,000-$19,000) compared to US standards but high education level (the majority have a 4-year degree and above).
The group has an average coding experience of 6 years and, on average, reported spending 9 hours on coding in a working day.
arms: The treated group was instructed to watch a 1-minute video introducing them to GitHub Copilot.
In addition to the instructions, they also received an automated email with installation instructions for GitHub Copilot once granted access to the tool.

We verify from telemetry after the experiment that all participants from the treated group have configured GitHub Copilot and accepted recommendations other than five who did not finish the sign up and thus started the experiment without the GitHub Copilot.
Both treated and control groups were instructed to complete an entry survey to provide demographic information such as age, gender, location, and educational background.
The control group did not have access to GitHub Copilot but was otherwise unconstrained, i.e., they were free to use internet search and Stack Overflow to complete the task.
outcome_definitions: We calculated two metrics as a measure of performance for each group: task success and task completion time.
Task success was measured as the percentage of participants in a group that adequately completed the task.
Task completion time was measured as the time from start to end of the task.
The creation date and time of that personal copy created a timestamp.
The full history of test suite runs is visible on each repository, enabling researchers to observe partial results for participants that did not fully complete the task.
statistical_results: The treatment group, with access to the AI pair programmer, completed the task 55.8% faster than the control group.
the treated group completed the task 55.8% faster (95% confidence interval: 21-89%).
Conditioning on completing the task, the average completion time from the treated group is 71.17 minutes and 160.89 minutes for the control group.
This represents a 55.8% reduction in completion time.
The p-value for the t-test is 0.0017, and a 95% confidence interval for the improvement is between [21%, 89%].
There are four outliers with time to completion above 300 min.
All outliers are in the control group, however our results remain robust if these outliers are dropped.

We also find that the treated group's success rate is 7 percentage points higher than the control group, but the estimate is not statistically significant, with a 95% confidence interval of [-0.11, 0.25].

The results show that less experienced developers (years of professional coding), developers with heavy coding load (hours of coding per day), and older developers (developers aged between 25 and 44) benefit more from Copilot.
Programming experience (years): Estimate 8.23, SE 4.36, t-Stat 1.90, p-Value 0.0629
Hours of programming per day: Estimate -11.70, SE 4.74, t-Stat -2.47, p-Value 0.0168
Age: 25-44: Estimate -74.55, SE 33.52, t-Stat -2.22, p-Value 0.0303
Unemployed: Estimate -35.98, SE 36.33, t-Stat -0.99, p-Value 0.3263
Income less than $20,000: Estimate 0.64, SE 27.47, t-Stat 0.02, p-Value 0.9814
No college: Estimate -36.57, SE 32.89, t-Stat -1.11, p-Value 0.2711
Language Preference: Java: Estimate -11.77, SE 33.16, t-Stat -0.35, p-Value 0.7240
Language Preference: Python: Estimate 22.90, SE 42.19, t-Stat 0.54, p-Value 0.5895
On average, participants in both treated and control groups estimated a 35% increase in productivity, which is an underestimation compared with the 55.8% increase in their revealed productivity.
The average irrelevant price for the treated group is $27.25, and the average irrelevant price for the control group is $16.91, both per month.
The difference is statistically significant at the 95% level.
ambiguity_or_caveats: This study examines a standardized programming task in an experiment to obtain a precise measure of productivity, instead of a task where developers collaborate on large projects in professional proprietary and/or open-source settings.
Productivity benefits may vary across specific tasks and programming languages, so more research is needed to understand how our results generalizes to other tasks.
Finally, this study does not examine the effects of AI on code quality.
AI assistance can increase code quality if it suggests code better than the programmer writes, or it can reduce quality if the programmer pays less attention to code.
The code quality can have performance and security considerations that can change the real-world impact of AI.
It is, as of yet, unclear how such gains would be distributed and how job tasks would change to incorporate AI-powered developer tools.

01a_quotes.notes: This is a controlled randomized experiment comparing GitHub Copilot (treatment) vs. no Copilot (control) on task completion time for an HTTP server implementation task. The study recruited 95 developers from Upwork, with 45 in treatment and 50 in control; 35 completed both task and survey. Primary outcome is task completion time. The paper reports heterogeneous treatment effects by developer experience, daily coding hours, age, employment status, income, education, and language preference. Exit survey data on self-reported productivity gains and willingness-to-pay are also reported.

01_extraction.title: The Impact of AI on Developer Productivity: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
01_extraction.study_design_description: A controlled randomized experiment was conducted from May 15, 2022 to June 20, 2022 to measure the productivity impact of GitHub Copilot. Ninety-five professional programmers recruited through Upwork were randomly assigned to control and treatment groups (45 treated, 50 control), with 35 completing both the task and survey. The experiment used GitHub Classroom to administer a standardized task and precisely measure timing and completion.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Recruited software developers were asked to implement an HTTP server in JavaScript as quickly as possible. The treatment group used GitHub Copilot to complete the task, while the control group did not. Participants received a personal copy of a template repository with task description and skeleton codebase, along with a test suite comprising twelve checks for submission correctness. Participants could use any sources of information (internet search, Stack Overflow) as they normally do. Task completion time was measured from repository creation to the first commit that successfully passed all 12 tests.
01_extraction.population_description: A total of 95 developers were recruited through Upwork and randomly assigned into control and treated groups. Most participants were in the age group of 25-34 and came from India and Pakistan, characterized by relatively lower income (median yearly income between $10,000-$19,000) but high education level (majority with a 4-year degree and above). The group had an average coding experience of 6 years and reported spending 9 hours on coding in a working day on average.

arms.description: The treated group was instructed to watch a 1-minute video introducing them to GitHub Copilot. They received an automated email with installation instructions for GitHub Copilot once granted access to the tool. Telemetry verified that all participants from the treated group configured GitHub Copilot and accepted recommendations other than five who did not finish the sign up and thus started the experiment without the GitHub Copilot.
The control group did not have access to GitHub Copilot but was otherwise unconstrained, i.e., they were free to use internet search and Stack Overflow to complete the task.

outcomes.outcome_description: Task completion time was measured as the time from start to end of the task. The time elapsed between the timestamp of repository creation and the timestamp of the first commit to successfully pass all 12 tests was counted as the participant's task completion time.
Task success was measured as the percentage of participants in a group that adequately completed the task.
Participants in both treated and control groups estimated their productivity increase.
Average irrelevant price (willingness to pay) for GitHub Copilot per month.

reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: The treatment group, with access to the AI pair programmer, completed the task 55.8% faster than the control group. ... the treated group completed the task 55.8% faster (95% confidence interval: 21-89%). ... The p-value for the t-test is 0.0017, and a 95% confidence interval for the improvement is between [21%, 89%].

Conditioning on completing the task, the average completion time from the treated group is 71.17 minutes and 160.89 minutes for the control group. This represents a 55.8% reduction in completion time.

We also find that the treated group's success rate is 7 percentage points higher than the control group, but the estimate is not statistically significant, with a 95% confidence interval of [-0.11, 0.25].
Programming experience (years): Estimate 8.23, SE 4.36, t-Stat 1.90, p-Value 0.0629

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: heterogeneous effect: hours of programming per day
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Hours of programming per day: Estimate -11.70, SE 4.74, t-Stat -2.47, p-Value 0.0168
Income less than $20,000: Estimate 0.64, SE 27.47, t-Stat 0.02, p-Value 0.9814
No college: Estimate -36.57, SE 32.89, t-Stat -1.11, p-Value 0.2711
Language Preference: Java: Estimate -11.77, SE 33.16, t-Stat -0.35, p-Value 0.7240
Language Preference: Python: Estimate 22.90, SE 42.19, t-Stat 0.54, p-Value 0.5895
On average, participants in both treated and control groups estimated a 35% increase in productivity, which is an underestimation compared with the 55.8% increase in their revealed productivity.

The average irrelevant price for the treated group is $27.25, and the average irrelevant price for the control group is $16.91, both per month. The difference is statistically significant at the 95% level.

01_extraction.notes: Study examined a standardized programming task in a controlled experiment rather than real-world collaborative development. Productivity benefits may vary across specific tasks and programming languages. The study does not examine effects of AI on code quality. Five participants in the treated group did not complete GitHub Copilot setup and started the experiment without the tool. Four outliers with completion time above 300 minutes were all in the control group; results remain robust if outliers are dropped. Thirty-five of 95 recruited developers completed both task and survey. Self-reported productivity gains (35%) were lower than revealed productivity gains (55.8%).

02_method.rationale: The study is described as 'A controlled randomized experiment' where 'professional programmers recruited through Upwork were randomly assigned to control and treatment groups.' This is randomized assignment but conducted online through Upwork and GitHub Classroom, not in a controlled lab or real workplace setting, making it a standard RCT.

per_outcome.operationalization: Time in minutes from repository creation timestamp to first commit passing all 12 tests, measured by platform timestamps
Percentage of participants who successfully completed the task by passing all 12 tests
Participant self-reported estimate of their own productivity increase, expressed as percentage
Average self-reported willingness to pay for GitHub Copilot subscription per month in dollars

04_onet.rationale: The participants are software developers implementing an HTTP server in JavaScript—a specific, well-defined programming task. The study measures their productivity on this discrete coding activity (writing code to pass 12 tests), not their performance across the full breadth of software development work. This narrow focus on 'implementing an HTTP server' maps best to the generalized work activity of 'Working with Computers' rather than to the broader Software Developer occupation, which encompasses requirements gathering, design, testing, deployment, and maintenance.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Only one AI arm (GitHub Copilot) is present in the study; control group had no AI assistance.
llm_extracted.notes: The paper reports task completion time in minutes (smaller is better). The treatment group completed the task 55.8% faster, which represents a reduction in time. The 95% CI [21%, 89%] refers to the percent reduction/improvement. Means are clearly reported: control = 160.89 min, copilot = 71.17 min. Multiple heterogeneous effect regression coefficients are reported but these are interaction terms, not the main effect, so they are not used for the primary log ratio calculation.
source_quotes: The treatment group, with access to the AI pair programmer, completed the task 55.8% faster than the control group.
the treated group completed the task 55.8% faster (95% confidence interval: 21-89%)
Conditioning on completing the task, the average completion time from the treated group is 71.17 minutes and 160.89 minutes for the control group.
This represents a 55.8% reduction in completion time.
The p-value for the t-test is 0.0017, and a 95% confidence interval for the improvement is between [21%, 89%].

computed.notes: The paper reports task completion time in minutes (smaller is better). The treatment group completed the task 55.8% faster, which represents a reduction in time. The 95% CI [21%, 89%] refers to the percent reduction/improvement. Means are clearly reported: control = 160.89 min, copilot = 71.17 min. Multiple heterogeneous effect regression coefficients are reported but these are interaction terms, not the main effect, so they are not used for the primary log ratio calculation. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Single AI intervention arm (GitHub Copilot) compared to control group without AI access.
llm_extracted.notes: After thorough re-read of the full paper, the task success outcome remains insufficiently reported for Hedges' g calculation. The paper states: 'We also find that the treated group's success rate is 7 percentage points higher than the control group, but the estimate is not statistically significant, with a 95% confidence interval of [-0.11, 0.25].' Only the raw difference (7 percentage points) and CI are provided. No means, SDs, SEs, test statistics (t, F), or standardized effect sizes are reported for this outcome. The p-value is described only as 'not statistically significant' with no exact value. The CI bounds [-0.11, 0.25] appear to be for the proportion difference itself, not a standardized effect. Without at least one of: (a) means+SDs, (b) means+SEs, (c) t-statistic+df, (d) F-statistic, (e) reported d/g, or (f) exact p-value with sufficient detail, Hedges' g cannot be computed. The primary outcome in this study is task completion time (55.8% reduction, p=0.0017), not task success rate, which is reported as a secondary finding with minimal statistical detail.
source_quotes: We also find that the treated group's success rate is 7 percentage points higher than the control group, but the estimate is not statistically significant, with a 95% confidence interval of [-0.11, 0.25].
Task success was measured as the percentage of participants in a group that adequately completed the task.
Thirty-five developers from both the treated and control groups completed the task and survey.

computed.notes: After thorough re-read of the full paper, the task success outcome remains insufficiently reported for Hedges' g calculation. The paper states: 'We also find that the treated group's success rate is 7 percentage points higher than the control group, but the estimate is not statistically significant, with a 95% confidence interval of [-0.11, 0.25].' Only the raw difference (7 percentage points) and CI are provided. No means, SDs, SEs, test statistics (t, F), or standardized effect sizes are reported for this outcome. The p-value is described only as 'not statistically significant' with no exact value. The CI bounds [-0.11, 0.25] appear to be for the proportion difference itself, not a standardized effect. Without at least one of: (a) means+SDs, (b) means+SEs, (c) t-statistic+df, (d) F-statistic, (e) reported d/g, or (f) exact p-value with sufficient detail, Hedges' g cannot be computed. The primary outcome in this study is task completion time (55.8% reduction, p=0.0017), not task success rate, which is reported as a secondary finding with minimal statistical detail. | arm pair unresolvable
